message:
docs(artifacts): E5 sixth-slice comparator capture and decision-record updates

Attach Track-E comparator evidence and advance the E5 decision record
through its sixth slice (asupersync-36m6p).

New artifacts:
- raptorq_track_e_gf256_bench_v1.json: baseline/auto/rollback
  comparator capture for gf256_primitives scenario RQ-E-GF256-005.
  Auto mode regresses addmul_slices2_fused by -22.8% median vs
  baseline; rollback restores baseline-level performance. Includes
  three-check rollback rehearsal (all pass).
- raptorq_track_e_gf256_p95p99_v1.json: derived p95/p99 percentile
  summaries across five scenarios and four operations per mode.
  Auto p99 tail heavier (9.57us vs 7.76us baseline); rollback
  restores p95/p99 below baseline. Flagged as directional pending
  larger-sample confirmation.

Decision record updates:
- Add E5 history entries for slices 4-6 with manifest snapshot API,
  environment metadata, and comparator artifact references
- Add evidence refs: comments #1848/#1855, coord thread messages
  #1408/#1410/#1422/#1424/#1441/#1443
- Add cargo check --all-targets to validation commands
- Remove compile-mismatch pending blocker (resolved in prior commit)
- Remaining E5 blocker: final full-corpus p95/p99 comparator evidence

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

+  "findings": [
+    "Auto mode shows a heavier p99 tail in the selected operation set versus baseline (9.5736 us vs 7.7600 us in this corpus).",
+    "Rollback corpus restores overall p95/p99 time tail below baseline in this sample set (6.9451/7.0658 us vs 7.3862/7.7600 us).",
+    "Per-op tails remain mixed across modes; this artifact should be treated as directional evidence pending larger-sample p95/p99 confirmation."
+  ],
+  "limitations": [
+    "Percentiles are computed over scenario-level medians rather than raw Criterion sample streams.",
+    "Per-operation percentile groups have n=5, so p95/p99 collapse to max by nearest-rank definition.",
+    "Runs used short Criterion windows (sample-size 10, 0.1s warmup/measurement), favoring fast iteration over statistical power."
+  ]
+}
